summary: DHH argues that speed of execution depends not just on competence but on risk tolerance, and most companies dramatically overestimate the criticality of their work. He presents a 'criticality ladder' ranging from life-threatening systems down to minor inconveniences, arguing that most software falls far closer to the low end. Using examples from Facebook's early 'move fast and break things' ethos and Basecamp's own evolution, he contends that organizations naturally slow down as they grow, often adding more process than the actual stakes warrant. The core message: even established companies should bias toward speed over excessive caution.
key_points: Risk tolerance matters as much as competence for development speed — you can't move fast if you treat every problem like rocket surgery
Most software sits far down the criticality ladder, closer to 'inconvenience' than 'loss of human life,' and should be treated accordingly
Executive overreaction to small mistakes causes organizations to become lethargic by discouraging appropriate risk-taking
Organizations naturally slow down as they grow, adding process that may be real or imagined in necessity — the trick is telling the difference
Young entrepreneurs have an advantage in risk-taking through 'bliss of ignorance,' while experienced veterans bring needed caution for high-stakes work
money_quotes: You can't go quickly if you treat every problem with the diligence required for rocket surgery.
The price you pay for making a mess is an instant surcharge on both quality and productivity.
That's the nature of the criticality bet. You need to have the stomach to keep playing, even if you occasionally lose a hand.

If executive wrath bears down upon you after any little mistake, you'll naturally just stop taking the appropriate risk, lest you tempt a small chance of getting chewed out. Organizations that work like this in the face of criticality that does not warrant it become lethargic.
We need youngsters blessed with the bliss of ignorance to push the envelope where the stakes are lower. And then some old-timers weathered by wisdom to pull the envelope when the stakes tip large.
Speed of execution is one of the most important organizational features in most areas of competition.

But if you must err, I contend that most companies, even us – two decades in and hundreds of millions in revenue later – are closer to Zuckerberg's sweet spot of moving fast and breaking things than we are to the rocket surgery.
key_insight: Most companies overestimate the criticality of their work and should bias toward speed over excessive caution, because the real risk is usually going too slow, not breaking things.
